title: Filipino Chicken Adobo

subs.name: 800 g pork belly cut into 3 cm cubes — increase braising to 35 minutes
subs.notes: pork adobo is equally traditional in the Philippines

ingredients.name: white vinegar (cane or rice vinegar preferred)
visual_checks: vinegar is the soul of adobo — do not reduce
ingredients.notes: vinegar is the soul of adobo — do not reduce

subs.name: 0.5 cup tamarind water (2 tbsp pulp dissolved in half cup water)
subs.notes: South Indian-friendly adaptation with a similar sour-salty result

subs.name: coconut aminos for a lower-sodium option
curry leaves for an Indian-pantry twist
subs.notes: gives a South Indian flavour bridge

ingredients.name: whole black peppercorns
visual_checks: do not substitute with ground pepper for the full aroma
ingredients.notes: do not substitute with ground pepper for the full aroma

ingredients.name: salt
visual_checks: taste first — soy sauce is already salty
ingredients.notes: taste first — soy sauce is already salty

steps.text: Combine the chicken pieces, vinegar, soy sauce, smashed garlic, bay leaves and peppercorns in a pot or kadai. Let everything marinate for 10 minutes at room temperature while you gather the rest of your equipment.

Bring the pot to a boil on medium-high heat, uncovered. Once boiling, reduce to a steady simmer and cook for 20–25 minutes, turning the chicken halfway, until the pieces are cooked through and the liquid has reduced by half.
Fish out the chicken pieces and set them aside. You should have about 1 cup of a dark, tangy braising liquid remaining — taste it. If too sour, add a pinch of sugar.
Heat oil in a separate wide pan or tawa on high. Fry the chicken pieces, skin-side down if applicable, for 3–4 minutes per side until browned and caramelised on the outside.

Pour the reduced braising sauce back over the chicken pieces in the pan. Simmer on medium heat for 3–5 more minutes, spooning the sauce over the chicken, until the sauce becomes a sticky, lacquered glaze that coats every piece.
Remove bay leaves. Serve over steamed rice, spooning the glossy garlic sauce from the pan over the top. Adobo refrigerates well for 3 days and the flavour intensifies each day.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: vinegar → 0.5 cup tamarind water (2 tbsp pulp dissolved in half cup water) (South Indian-friendly adaptation with a similar sour-salty result); chicken → 800 g pork belly cut into 3 cm cubes — increase braising to 35 minutes (pork adobo is equally traditional in the Philippines); soy-sauce → coconut aminos for a lower-sodium option (slightly sweeter flavour profile); bay-leaf → curry leaves for an Indian-pantry twist (gives a South Indian flavour bridge)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Chicken adobo is extremely easy to adapt in an Indian kitchen. White vinegar (sirka) is available at every grocery store. Use any heavy-bottomed kadai or saucepan for the braising step. Soy sauce is widely available. Whole peppercorns and bay leaves (tej patta) are standard Indian pantry items. The two-step braise-then-fry technique is familiar to anyone who has made Indian chicken curries.
